Aravind Srinivas
Perplexity•823K followers
1y
-
Excited to announce that we've raised 62.7M$ at 1.04B$ valuation, led by Daniel Gross, along with Stan Druckenmiller, NVIDIA, Jeff Bezos, Tobi Lutke, Garry Tan, Andrej Karpathy, Dylan Field, Elad Gil, Nat Friedman, IVP, NEA, Jakob Uszkoreit, Naval Ravikant, Brad Gerstner, and Lip-Bu Tan. We will use the additional funding to grow our usage across consumers and knowledge workers in enterprises. For consumers: we have inked partnerships with Deutsche Telekom and Softbank to distribute Perplexity to ~116M users worldwide. We earlier announced a similar partnership with SK Telecom at the Mobile World Congress in February. And for Enterprise: we've announced the launch of Perplexity Enterprise Pro, to bring the world's best AI answer engine into the hands of every knowledge worker in every organization: https://lnkd.in/egwSgGJX Daniel is a special investor. I really look up to him and his career. He was a founder and worked on a search engine called Cue, and sold the company to Apple, where he ran Spotlight Search and several other projects. He was also the first real beta-tester of http://Perplexity.ai prior to its launch in Dec 2022. Thanks for all the help and support from all our users so far! We look forward to continuing to accelerate and grow and to providing accurate knowledge and information to every person on the planet. Blog: https://lnkd.in/e_BE-4Qi Bloomberg: https://lnkd.in/edpK28dp

I faced an impossible choice: 60 days to create a strategy for Ecuador's $3.5 billion in agricultural exports to Germany. 12 competing products. Limited budget. A small team. And a compliance deadline that could lock 120 producers out of the EU market. The traditional answer? A $500k, 8-week consulting report. I had neither the time nor the money. So I built a decision simulation engine called TACTIK. It’s not another chatbot. It’s a flight simulator for high-stakes conversations that integrates the conflicting advice of multiple experts—transparently. Then, the ultimate test: I ran a simulation of a trade crisis my father faced 30 years ago as Ambassador to France. The result? TACTIK matched his 30 years of diplomatic experience with 95% accuracy. I wrote a full article breaking down the system, the 3 critical decisions I made, and how the validation against my father's experience worked. Read the full story here → [Link to the LinkedIn Article You Publish] #DecisionMaking #Strategy #AI #Leadership #Diplomacy #Simulation #AgriTech #SupplyChain #Ecuador #TACTIKAI https://docs.google.com/document/d/1-uViwL5dqk-_sXPxJMTGS07acwaD99wE68zy6m5WK7I/edit?usp=drivesdk

In a recent interview, Perplexity CEO Aravind Srinivas shared a powerful insight: due to the rapid pace of AI evolution, their company can only plan confidently up to three months ahead. The future is about being agile, staying curious, and embracing uncertainty with open arms. If we want to lead effectively in this new era, we need to prepare ourselves and our teams to think and move fast, adapt constantly, and find opportunity in change. Fear is the real barrier to AI innovation. When anxiety dominates the conversation around AI, it dampens creativity and collaboration. Leaders have a critical role in changing this narrative. By focusing on real-world successes, they can frame AI as a powerful tool for augmentation rather than a replacement. 🌟 Real-world Case Studies: Highlight examples where AI has improved outcomes without displacing workers. 🔧 Tools for Empowerment: Showcase how AI can elevate tasks, making routine processes more efficient and freeing up time for creative work. 🤝 Collaborative Environment: Cultivate a workspace where experimentation and risk-taking are encouraged, leading to breakthroughs. ✨ Fostering Hope: Shift conversations from a fear-based perspective to one filled with possibilities and potential. This approach not only motivates employees but also fosters an innovative culture essential for navigating the ever-evolving technological landscape. All these automated LLM browsing experiences are going down a rabbit hole, it is completely the wrong abstraction.
Humans use browsers for many reasons, and the UX and interface (mouse, clicking, visual) is optimized for humans.
Making LLMs adapt to this experience is adding noise and making the usage of LLMs less reliable.
They should go straight to the APIs of the services they are interacting with.

The Delight-Triggering Superpower
You know that buzz when an app just "gets" you? With intuitive micro-interactions that scratch an itch you didn't know you had? That's the superpower of transcendent UX - bridging digital interfaces and innate human instincts.
Whether recreating familiar physical cues or simplifying complexity into delightful flows, thoughtful UX activates our primal pleasure receptors. It transforms bland software into experience people obsessively love and crave more of.
That elusive quality defines the products we cherish for life versus those uninstalled in rage. Mastering the art of triggering user delight? That's the ultimate edge.

5moEdited
While I'm still coming closer to the UX itself, before it we have to understand the way how we proceed with the information. I talked about perception, now I want to talk about memory, which is important to keep in mind while you are thinking about an experience overall.
- Did you know that we forget about 40% of new information after only 20 minutes, and 70% just the next day?
- Or did you know that our brain can alter our memory? And we can remember things which never actually happened!
- Did you know that our working memory can hold about 4–5 items for adults and 2-3 items for children according to recent research?
- To remember something and transfer it to long-term memory, we have to record it in detail, through meaningful use or association.
- Also, emotion can make experiences and information more memorable.

There’s so much more to explore in DeepResearch—and surprisingly little discussion so far! 🔄 Excited to share our new work: "Understanding DeepResearch via Reports"! 📄 Huggingface: https://lnkd.in/ebBi4exE Arxiv: https://lnkd.in/eDenKnHC We hope it sparks discussion around how we build and evaluate DeepResearch systems. 🤔 🔍 Motivation: In DeepResearch, the final report—not just search results—is what truly matters to users. LLMs do not crawl the web, do not maintain indexes, and do not enforce ranking algorithms at internet scale. They operate as presentation and reasoning layers on top of the classic information retrieval (IR) pipeline. The recent paper Why Language Models Hallucinate (Kalai, Nachum, Vempala, Zhang, 2025) shows why this distinction matters: LLMs inevitably hallucinate due to statistical limits and evaluation incentives. Without grounding in real retrieval systems, they cannot provide reliable search. https://lnkd.in/gfFykiVR

Following my curiosity in…•2K followers
6mo Edited
Turns out, too many Agents spoil the broth, too! This week, I explored deep research agents, systems that simulate layered reasoning through iteration and reflection. I built a configurable multi-agent research agent, ran several configurations, and used an LLM-based evaluator to score their outputs. A single researcher agent iterating deeply performed better than multiple researcher agents working in parallel. Observations: - Deeper iteration builds a more coherent understanding. - Parallel agents explore more ideas, but they don’t reinforce or refine each other’s reasoning. - API rate limits wiped out most of the theoretical speed gains from parallel runs. How it works: A hierarchical supervisor–researcher setup: - The supervisor delegates sub-topics to researcher agents - Each researcher runs ReAct loops with web search and reflection tools - Configurable parameters control parallelism, iteration depth, and tool usage Video demo: https://lnkd.in/gS26pbnH Jupyter Notebook: https://lnkd.in/gs5NgW3a Thanks to AI Makerspace for another great week of learning!#AIE8

This week Anthropic released Claude Sonnet 4.5. Most people are talking about the coding improvements, but what really caught my attention is how the model now handles memory. Claude can now save information as Markdown files, which allows it to keep context from one session to the next. That shift means the model is no longer just reacting to prompts, it is starting to actually remember. Anthropic has been focused on helping models stay efficient by keeping only what truly matters and ignoring unnecessary details. I see this as a real step toward a more intelligent kind of reasoning, not just a bigger context window. OpenAI and Google are exploring similar ideas with memory management. For now, it still looks simple, like storing text, but in the future this could evolve into a network of relationships and meanings that works more like human memory. If that happens, organizations will need to think about how they structure their own knowledge so it can connect with these systems. And it will be important to ensure that memory remains open and accessible, not locked away in private ecosystems. What do you think about all this?

Today, we're launching the first version of Perplexity's Enterprise Search, called "Internal Knowledge Search". For a long time, people have wanted one single tool and platform to do all their… | Aravind Srinivas | 237 comments

Perplexity•837K followers
1y
Today, we're launching the first version of Perplexity's Enterprise Search, called "Internal Knowledge Search". For a long time, people have wanted one single tool and platform to do all their research at work. And research isn't restricted to just knowledge that lies internally; you want the insights from the web in the context of what's most relevant to your organization. Yet, the tools that supported external search and internal search were fragmented, separate, and built on completely different legacy tech stacks; making it difficult to have one consolidated tool. With the advent of LLMs, multi-step reasoning, and tool use; it is now possible to address this issue and offer a fresh take on all research within an enterprise: be it internal files or external web, and we're glad to pioneer this at Perplexity. Enterprise is not just about file access, but also collaboration and customization. We're also excited to launch "Perplexity Spaces", a simple way for you build research hubs where you can upload persistent files (to have them as additional sources), pick an AI model of your choice, write custom instructions for the type of response you want, and invite collaborators to share your threads. Collaboratively picking which files to add to a space lets you address a big issue in Enterprise Search: "ranking" - which files actually matter for the company; and we believe users picking them is a solid way to address duplication, incomplete / incorrect / outdated documents etc that aren't addressed with connectors. This feature is also available to regular users who want to build custom spaces for repeat use cases of Perplexity. Spaces is already available on the sidebar to all users; and existing Enterprise users should already be able to toggle between Web & Org mode for their sources and start uploading files on their Settings tab! Check out our blog post to learn more: https://pplx.ai/ieEXvh4

The current wave in AI isn’t about smarter chatbots — it’s about agentic systems: platforms not only generating content but acting autonomously to deliver outcomes. These are not assistants, they are co-builders of worlds — ecosystems where human vision is amplified through machine agency. A recent article on designing successful agentic AI systems reinforces three pivotal truths: 1. Define the world you’re building. Agentic systems require more than good models; they need a clear environment, goals and constraints. Without a defined “world” for the agent to inhabit, autonomy becomes confusion, not value. 2. Calibrate human-machine roles. When agencies drift, humans retreat into monitoring or abdication. To avoid this, define the ledger of who initiates, who oversees, who escalates. Designers must build human-agent teams where agency is shared yet accountable. 3. Governance is not an add-on—it’s the scaffolding. Autonomous action without guardrails is not freedom, it’s risk. As systems scale, governance must scale too: decision-rights, escalation paths, feedback loops, and ethical design become core architecture, not just legal compliance. In many organizations I traverse, the temptation is to treat “agentic AI” as a feature: a fancy button, a proof-of-concept. But that framing misses the magnitude. You’re not tweaking a tool: you’re constructing a new operational domain. That means building worlds in which people and machines co-inhabit — where machines act, humans guide, and the shared purpose is clear. Here’s a checklist for leaders who intend to be builders of worlds: • Vision anchor: What future state is the system enabling? Describe the world. • Role clarity: Who does what? Humans + agents + hybrid workflows. • Outcome metrics: How will success manifest in the world you’re creating? • Feedback infrastructure: What loops bring signals back into design? • Ethical & governance layer: What rights, risks, and remediation flows exist? • Change cadence: How will you iterate the world as agents learn? If your answer to “What world are we building?” is unclear, you shouldn’t build an agentic system yet. Because the inevitable result of mis-designed agency is another silo, another hidden cost, another fractured workflow. But if you get this right — you become not just a leader of projects — you become a world-builder. One who shapes the operating domain, aligns human and machine agency, and delivers a new terrain of value. Leadership today is not only about decisions made — it’s about domains created. And in the age of agentic AI, the domains we build will determine who leads.#AgenticAI#SystemsThinking#Leadership#OrganizationalTransformation#Innovation#AIstrategy#HumanMachineTeam#Design#FutureOfWork

Aravind Srinivas, CEO of Perplexity, had one goal when he got into IIT Madras: to study Computer Science. In his first semester, he pushed himself to achieve the grades needed to switch from Electrical Engineering to his dream field. But when results came, he missed the cutoff by just 0.01 points. Feeling defeated, Aravind found himself slipping into a shell, feeling like he didn’t quite belong. But instead of giving up, he threw himself into learning on his own. He discovered online programming courses and taught himself Python before it was even popular at IIT. It became a way to prove, even if only to himself, that he could do it. By his third year, he topped a tough machine learning course, surprising even his professors. His hard work paid off when AI pioneer Yoshua Bengio offered him an internship, eventually recommending him for a PhD. Aravind was accepted at UC Berkeley, where his work caught the attention of John Schulman at OpenAI. That led to an internship at OpenAI, a dream come true. After stints at DeepMind and Google, he returned to OpenAI, but soon decided to forge his own path. In 2022, he launched Perplexity AI, which is now valued at over $8B. That missed 0.01% in college had lit a fire that took him far beyond his dreams. If you love something, your coursework doesn't matter. You can still make it big.

Hello, Connections!
Here's a small test project I created as part of a technical assessment for a startup. Built using .NET 8 and C# with the MVC framework, this is a tipping system designed for businesses to manage and calculate daily tips based on employee shifts in a specific roster.
The project leverages the strategy pattern, a widely recognized design pattern in the industry for its flexibility and runtime interchangeability of algorithms.
This project uses good coding standards with implementing object oriented programing concepts like encapsulation, abstraction etc. Using .Net test framework, Unit test cases were also implemented as a part of the requirements mentioned in the assessment.

🌟What the heck is desistance? 🌟Desistance theory explores how individuals stop committing crimes and lead fulfilling lives. How do clients go from antisocial behaviors to prosocial behaviors? It’s not a quick process; it takes time and is influenced by various personal and social factors. This understanding helps community supervision officers and others in the criminal justice system support clients during reentry.
Desistance is a journey that includes three key stages:
Primary desistance: Stopping criminal behavior.
Secondary desistance: Adopting a non-offending identity.
Tertiary desistance: Gaining recognition and acceptance from others.
Come back tomorrow for tips to support your clients on their desistance journey ☀Sources:[https://lnkd.in/eQc3VbEP](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/eQc3VbEP&urlhash=dhiw&trk=public_post-text)[https://lnkd.in/e\_fDZtvP](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/e_fDZtvP&urlhash=w7K_&trk=public_post-text)

From Prison Yards to Purpose CEO of Wrong to Strong | Mentor | Mindset Coach

Fitness | Discipline | Resilience
👇Walk the Yard with Me
5mo
At 19 years old, I wasn’t like most teenagers. Instead of college dreams or carefree days, I was in shackles, being transported from Mexico to the United States. The kind of transfer reserved for dangerous criminals—armed guards escorting me off a plane like a threat to society. A kid, in their eyes, but to them, I was something else.
I’ll never forget the cold stares of the officers, the way they looked at me as if I were a monster, not a young man who had barely started living. That walk, under the watchful eyes of rifles and handguns, wasn’t just a physical journey across borders. It was a walk into a dark place in my mind, one where I started to believe that this is who I was supposed to be. It was as if every click of those shackles was sealing a part of my fate.
Imagine what it does to a 19-year-old kid’s mind—to be treated like an animal, like a danger that needs to be contained. It felt like I was stepping into a role they’d written for me, and I had no choice but to play it out. The hardest part wasn’t the flight or the guards—it was fighting the idea that this was all I was ever going to be.
Today, I share this not to glorify the past, but to show you how deep the scars can go and how far I’ve had to come. It’s easy to look at where I am now and think it was always this way, but it wasn’t. It took years of hard work to rewrite the narrative they tried to stamp on my life at 19.
From walking in chains to walking free, building a legacy of change. Your reaction and response can have a significant influence on how they make sense to what has happened to them: Listen, Believe and Support.
💜Listen: Let survivors know that you are there for them, and that their experience is real and valid.
💜Believe: Understand that a survivor’s experience will be complex and show up for them when they need your support.

Transcript: Perplexity CEO Srinivas on Winning Search With AI – The Singju Post
Home» Transcript: Perplexity CEO Srinivas on Winning Search With AI

- June 20, 2025 6:54 amJune 20, 2025 6:54 am
Read the full transcript of Aravind Srinivas, Co-Founder and CEO of Perplexity, in conversation with Bloomberg’s Shirin Ghaffary about the startup’s approach to reinventing online search, rising investor interest, and how Perplexity plans to stay ahead in the AI innovation race at Bloomberg Tech in San Francisco. (June 6, 2025)

SHIRIN GHAFFARY: You started Perplexity with the ambitious mission of taking on Google using AI. And what most people don’t know is that you were actually an intern at Google DeepMind in 2019. It was your first job after studying for your PhD at UC Berkeley. Go Bears. Did you know when you were interning at Google that you would just a few years later start a multibillion dollar startup going against it?
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Definitely not. But I did everything an intern would do, just sleep in the office. They had all these pods which you can use to sleep in the office. They had a library. They had a lot of food, a lot of junk food too, which, you know, definitely I put on a lot of weight while I was in London.
And so it was one of the best times I had in my life. I think there’s a lot of things I would say. DeepMind was almost like a different company. I even remember how Demis had all these crazy visions of how AI will just be a scientist by itself and it’ll solve all theorems and proofs. And they were on a completely different trajectory.
So now they got folded back into Google to get them back into this AI race on the product stuff. But to me, DeepMind meant a lot more in terms of just going for that core artificial general intelligence dream. It was a fantastic time.

SHIRIN GHAFFARY: So you went on to work at OpenAI in its early days and then eventually decided to break out and start Perplexity. And for those who don’t know, can you tell us a little bit about what Perplexity is?
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: So Perplexity is an answer machine. You get answers that you can really trust. We were the first to reimagine search in the form of conversations and answers with trusted sources. And we did it in a way that essentially became the blueprint for every other chatbot out there.
Yes, everybody else has integrated search too, but when we started, people made fun of us, saying, in AI, hallucination is meant to be a feature, not a bug. And so I even had a former Googler who was one of my early seed investors, and he said, this looks good, but the reason ChatGPT is going viral now is because people want to laugh at AI’s mistakes.
And your product is trying to design it in a way where you do not make mistakes because you’re always pulling sources and summarizing what humans are saying. You’re not telling what th...

Aravind Srinivas: Perplexity's Race to Build Agentic Search (Transcript) – The Singju Post
Home» Aravind Srinivas: Perplexity’s Race to Build Agentic Search (Transcript)

- July 17, 2025 3:33 am
Read the full transcript of Aravind Srinivas, CEO of Perplexity in conversation with General Partner of Y Combinator David Lieb on “Perplexity’s Race to Build Agentic Search” at AI Startup School in San Francisco on June 16, 2025.

DAVID LIEB: Aravind, I see you every two or three months and you give me an update on the latest on Perplexity. Why don’t you just tell these folks where you’re at? How are things going? Do people use Perplexity? Do you guys use Perplexity?
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Well, whether you believe it or not, I have infrastructure issues every day. So there are a lot of people using it and this usage is actually growing to the extent that we don’t actually know how to deal with it. We have to rebuild the infrastructure to scale the next 10x. So definitely a lot of people in the world using it. Thanks to all of you as well.
What is next for us? The browser. That’s the big bet we are making as far as the future of the company goes. Everyone here is like, “Why should I use Perplexity when there’s search and other AI apps?” Of course ChatGPT has a bigger distribution than us. Every other AI app is trying to put search as a layer in it. All of them support citations, a lot of them support some of the verticals we have put work into.
Yes, we’re always going to continue to remain better than others in that category, but I think the browser and agents are truly the next bet that we want to make. We think about it as an assistant rather than a complete autonomous agent, but one omnibox where you can navigate, you can ask informational queries and you can give agentic tasks and your AI with you on your new tab page, on your sidecar, as an assistant, on any webpage you are makes the browser feel like more like a cognitive operating system rather than just yet another browser.

And we hope to make it like a cloud where you launch several tasks in parallel that are running asynchronously and pulling all your personal contacts, your email, your calendar, your Amazon, all sorts of social media accounts that you have and you go and do research on real estate, the markets, and these are all just processes running on your browser. That’s never been possible before. And Chrome was exciting when each tab was its own process. We think about each query or each prompt could be that. And that will be our new browser comment. So we’re putting all our energy into that.
DAVID LIEB: This was going to be the hard question I saved for the end, but since you queued it up, I’ll do it right now. I think if Sam Altman were still on the stage today, he would say, “Oh, yeah, that’s what we’re doing.” And I think Sundar at Google proba...

Transcript: Perplexity CEO Aravind Srinivas’ Interview on Figuring Out With Raj Shamani – The Singju Post

- August 7, 2025 1:34 am
Read the full transcript of Perplexity CEO Aravind Srinivas’ interview on Figuring Out With Raj Shamani podcast episode # FO349 titled “How To Build Billion Dollar AI Business – Elon Musk, Google & Future”, May 1, 2025.

RAJ SHAMANI: When you think about India or just like, let’s say world, and you want to compete and you want to be the best, you want to be number one. Why keep name as Perplexity? It’s so difficult to say. I was talking to so many people in the organization because I use Perplexity and there are two, three people who use Perplexity. And then when we spoke to everyone that, hey, you know what, we’re going to get the founder, we’ll do this. And they’re like, it’s such a difficult name. We’re never going to be able to even download it and use it. And it’s an important question.
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Yeah. So this goes back to the origins of the company. When I started, I didn’t really have a clear sense of whether we’ll build a search app or consumer application. None of that. We knew we wanted to do something in search. We knew we wanted to build unique experiences that were not possible before. We were not dreaming big, like, oh, we’re going to build a billion user product potentially.
So when you pick a name for the company, you pick something pretty cool, and easy to get a domain for. I didn’t have much money at the beginning of funding, so Perplexity AI was available for like $120 for two years. So I just got it. And Perplexity is a metric in AI that measures how well you understand something. So very geeky. I get it. But once you get even more users and more funding, we can just buy the rights to some crazy name and just do something like four or five letters. That would be amazing.
RAJ SHAMANI: Interesting. Okay, so there is plan to actually change it. This is a problem you’re aware about?
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Yeah, yeah. I mean, look, it’s one of the worst names you can pick for a consumer company. 100% aware of it. And I’m not going to defend. Maybe we can do something like the company will still be called Perplexity, but the product can be called something else.

RAJ SHAMANI: I was looking at the list of your investors. Jeff Bezos happens to be one of them.
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Yeah, he came much later. He’s not the earliest investor.
RAJ SHAMANI: Okay, but did you meet Jeff?
ARAVIND SRINIVAS: Yeah, I did meet Jeff.
RAJ SHAMANI: How did that happen? How did you go from being this guy who doesn’t know much about this business genius and how this model will make sense to getting Jeff Bezos investing in you?

Limitless Podcast - Aravind Srinivas: Perplexity CEO’s All-In Gamble to Take Down Google Transcript and Discussion

Episode Date: August 18, 2025
Starting point is 00:00:03 We are here with Arvind Serenovas from Perplexity. Arvind, welcome to Limitless. Thank you for having me. Arvind, we want to learn something from you. We generally understand on Limitless that the future is going to be very different from what it looks like today, most likely starting with the internet. So we want to hear how you think the internet will be different in, you know, five, five years. And how that thesis of yours has informed your leadership of your company perplexity. It's never easy to imagine the world like five years from now.
Starting point is 00:00:33 even in normal circumstances. And we live in like a pretty different world where AI is advancing at a pace that we just not used to. So I would be lying if I said I really know how the world's going to look like Fides and I actually don't think anybody really does. Because it's so hard to work around scenarios where the capabilities of AI are constantly evolving every few months.
Starting point is 00:01:00 Three months before all day, agentic capabilities where chain of actions and chain of tool calls were not reliable. We're not like, like, you could, you could see like the contours of it, but it was not as well defined as it is today. TRANSCRIPT:

ARAVIND SRINIVAS: There are a couple of ways I’m not a traditional tech founder. I never dropped out of college. In fact, I kept going. I’m an academic, you could say. And it’s OK to be proud that I have a PhD in AI from Berkeley, right here in the Bay Area.

But there’s something interesting in AI that I’ve noticed, compared to other tech founders. Other stereotypes, at least. A lot of us hold PhDs. I mean, quite a lot. 11 out of 24 speakers just at this conference have PhDs, and over a third are assistant, associate or full professors with major universities.

Only time will tell if this is a new trend of seeing academics in technology startups. But I got pretty curious to find out if this is common or new. And it turns out this is somewhat new. Only over a year ago, researchers at the University of Maryland found a 38 percent decline at the rate of startup formation or share of employment by US PhDs over the past 20 years. Yet our attendance here today and the trend in AI technology broadly does not seem to correlate with this finding. As I said, only time and more data will tell.

In the meantime, my curiosity led me to another question: What was the last major technology company founded by academics? Google.

At Perplexity, we get accused of trying to kill Google a lot.

Perplexity’s Aravind Srinivas on the Infinite Value of Knowledge | Stanford Graduate School of Business

Perplexity’s Aravind Srinivas on the Infinite Value of Knowledge | Stanford Graduate School of Business

2 min read

The co-founder and CEO of Perplexity discusses building the world’s first answer engine on View From The Top: The Podcast.

By courtesy

June 18, 2025

| by

Aravind Srinivas leads Perplexity, a startup whose AI-powered search engine provides direct, sourced answers to any question you might ask it.

Srinivas joined Aislin Roth, MBA ’25, on View From The Top, for a candid conversation that touched on his philosophy, Perplexity’s business model, and the challenges of building a leading-edge AI company. From rounding up investors — including camping out in front of Yann LeCun’s office — to fighting the inertia that grips startups as they grow, Srinivas provides unique insight into how a young leader steers a late-stage startup with big aspirations.

“If we are a reliable answer machine to everybody and widely accessible, that not just gives you answers but helps you accomplish tasks, too — make transactions, buy things, book things, book flights, get the best deals and make your life more productive, give you back more time — I think we are going to be a pretty industry-defining product and company,” he says.

That’s because knowledge is more valuable than wealth, Srinivas says.

“You can probably focus on wealth and your net worth, you can try to use that as a metric for your progress in life, but at some point it taps out. On the other hand, there is no end to knowledge. That’s why at Perplexity the tagline is ‘where knowledge begins,’ because there’s actually truly no end to knowledge and you can only keep getting better.”

Stanford GSB’s View From The Top is the dean’s premier speaker series. It launched in 1978 and is supported in part by the F. Kirk Brennan Speaker Series Fund.

Perplexity AI CEO Aravind Srinivas, PhD 21, on why he ditched pitch ...

Perplexity AI CEO Aravind Srinivas, PhD 21, on why he ditched pitch decks - Haas News | UC Berkeley Haas

By

Stella Kotik

After years interning and working as a researcher at companies like Google, DeepMind, and OpenAI, Aravind Srinivas, PhD 21 (computer science), realized he needed to be his own boss.

“Some people are unemployable. They just don’t listen to what the boss tells them to do,” Srinivas co-founder, president, and CEO of Perplexity AI, joked at a recent Dean’s Speaker Series talk. “I’m one of them.”

Now, he said, Perplexity’s customers are the boss. “‘This is not good.’ ‘That’s not good.’’’ Every day I wake up, my phone has hundreds of these (messages) because I see across all these different social platforms, and that’s how I know, every day, there’s some work to do.”

Founded in 2022, Perplexity’s chatbot search engine now logs more than 300 million queries a week. With a mission to “serve the world’s curiosity,” the company recently released Comet, an AI-powered web browser that integrates an AI assistant directly into its users browsing experience.

Srinivas’ path toward entrepreneurship began during his PhD in computer science at UC Berkeley. He was drawn to the idea that both business and computer science start with an idea that you have to convince others to believe in, he said, whether that’s through writing a research grant or pitching to investors. “I’ve always looked up to entrepreneurs,” he said. “Driving change the way you think should be done can only be truly done as an entrepreneur.”

Srinivas said what distinguishes Perplexity from legacy search engines isn’t its infrastructure but its approach toward search. Rather than rely on keywords, ads, or clickbait, the platform aims to answer questions with direct, reliable answers. Aravind Srinivas's 4 Rules for Winning in Tech

Aravind Srinivas's 4 Rules for Winning in Tech 

 

 

 

You're reading Entrepreneur India, an international franchise of Entrepreneur Media.

Aravind Srinivas, Co-founder and CEO of Perplexity, still swears by one classic mantra: “Work incredibly hard and there’s no substitute for it. Don’t overthink or act like you’re too smart, trying to strategically hack your way to building a company despite everything.”

But he also has a word of caution for young entrepreneurs. If your product succeeds and starts generating millions or even billions in revenue, others, especially large AI labs will likely copy your model.

“You should assume that if your company becomes a big hit, if it’s making hundreds of millions or potentially billions in revenue, model companies will copy it. These labs are raising tens of billions, and they need to justify all that capex. They’re constantly looking for new monetisation avenues. So they’ll copy anything that works.”

Embrace the fear of getting copied

Rather than being paralysed by fear, Srinivas urges aspiring entrepreneurs to embrace it and use it as fuel. “You have to live with it. Your moat comes from moving fast and building a unique identity. At the end of the day, users care about specificity like when you’re hiring someone for house help, you want a specific person, not a general agency.”

“Sleep with that fear, wake up excited to build. That’s the only thing that’ll keep you going.”

On the innovator’s dilemma: what if you were google?

When asked what he’d do if he were in Sundar Pichai’s shoes, facing the choice to cannibalise existing products to build a Perplexity competitor, Srinivas offered a candid perspective. “I can say what I want, but they have more data on what users are doing. Also, there are a lot of people who still hate AI. Pushing AI across a massive distribution network like Google’s isn’t easy.” “What would I do? Aravind Srinivas is the CEO of Perplexity, the conversational "answer engine" that provides precise, user-focused answers to queries — with in-line citations. Aravind co-founded the company in 2022 after working as a research scientist at OpenAI, Google, and DeepMind. To date, Perplexity has raised over $165 million from investors including Jeff Bezos, Nat Friedman, Elad Gil, NVIDIA, and the late Susan Wojciki. He has a PhD in computer science from UC Berkeley and a Bachelors and Masters in Electrical Engineering from the Indian Institute of Technology, Madras.

In this fireside chat at Stripe, Perplexity cofounder and CEO Aravind Srinivas discusses how Perplexity is building a new AI-powered alternative to traditional search engines, their journey so far, the tech behind it, and the rapidly evolving digital search landscape.

Hi everyone,
thank you so much for joining us and a very warm welcome
to our special guest today, Aravind Srinivas of Perplexity AI.
You're CEO.
Really excited to have
a rich conversation here, and I'd first like to learn a bit more
about Perplexity myself, and then we'll open up
for some Q&A from the audience.
So Aravind,
tell us a little bit about the journey.
Why did you start Perplexity?
It's an AI-powered search engine...
lots of search engines out there, and what's going on at the company today?
-Yeah, thank you all for coming here.
We started Perplexity about
one and a half years ago, definitely not to build
a new search alternative.
We're incredibly audacious,
and I wish I was that audacious, but that's not the reality.
We started very precisely
to focus on one particular problem of building a great natural language
to SQL-2.
We were very motivated and inspired
by search engines and Google's story because we are also academics
becoming entrepreneurs and that was the only example
that we could look at.
So that flowed into how we
approached the SQL problem.
We didn't build a SQL prod solution
as a coding copilot, but rather as a searching over databases
sort of a tool.
And one of the tools we built,
one of the prototypes we built, was actually something
relevant to Stripe.
We looked at: how would people
do analytics over their Stripe data using Stripe Sigma?
And we built this natural language,
the Stripe Sigma tool, because it was some version of Presto,
and not everybody knows how to write it.
One of our investors, Nat Friedman,
was actually using it to do some analytics of
his own Stripe data.
So all that was very exciting for us, but we were never finding any big dopamine
or traction from real usage.
It was just a few hundreds of queries
a week, and we decided, okay,
nobody is going to give us their data if we are like a random startup.
Nobody knows anything about us.
So we just had to scrape external data and build a cool demo at scale,
and maybe they'll look at it, and then they would give us some data.
And so we did that by
scraping all of Twitter.
We built this thing called Bird-SQL.
We called it Bird-SQL
because we are not allowed to use the Twitter name due to trademark.
But it was just literally
scraping all of Twitter, organizing it into a bunch of tables,
and powering search over that.
And that worked really well, and that's how we got all
of our initial investors.
All that somewhat inspired by how Stripe,
like Patrick and John, raised money.
They would show the demo to people
and get these cool angels, like Peter Thiel or Elon Musk.
If you look at Stripe's
angel investors list, it's pretty amazing.
So that's how we got a bunch
of cool investors, including Jeff Dean.
He tried our Twitter search demo,
and he was like, "I've never used something
like this before, and I really like it." At that time he did not see anything
similar to what we were doing today, which is why now we don't openly say
he's an investor, because of the conflict.
But as we progressed,
we just kept realizing that all the work we did
of taking external data, processing it,
putting it into structured tables, and then having the LLMs do the search, can be changed into doing
very little offline work in terms of pre-processing
and letting the LLMS do more of the work on post-processing
at inference time.
Because LLMs were getting smarter,
we could see that.
We started off with
very old GPT-3 models and Codex.
And as GPT-3.5 came, DaVinci-002,
or DaVinci-003, and Turbo, we could just see that they were getting
cheaper, faster, and better.
So we switched our strategy,
and we were like, okay, try to just get the links, and try to get the raw
data from those links, and try to do more work
at inference time online.
And this plays to a new kind of advantage
that Google is not built for.
Google is built for all the work you do
in the pre-processing step.
That's their bread and butter,
nobody can defeat them there.
But for the first time,
you don't need to do all of that.
You do need to do some of that still
for efficiency and speed, but not as much as they've done
over the last two decades.
And so we rolled out this generic search that just took links and summarized it
in the form of citations, and we put it out as a disclaimer, "Hey, you know what, this is a cool demo
that's daisy-chaining GPT-3.5 and Bing, and we want to work with bigger companies,
so please reach out to us at this email." We're just still trying to do enterprise.
And we did get emails.
We got emails from HP and Dell asking, "Hey, how would it look like if you used
something like this for our data?" But what also ended up happening
is our usage was sustaining.
It was not just like an initial spike
and then nobody cared.
And then we decided, okay,
let's take another step, let's make it conversational,
so that you can ask a follow-up based on the past query
and the past links, and it will retain the context.
That's an experience nobody
has shown so far, including ChatGPT.
ChatGPT had nothing
related to web browsing or anything like that at the time.
And then our usage just
kept growing week after week after week without any marketing,
pure word of mouth.
So we just decided, okay,
this is good enough to work on.
It's pretty exciting.
None of us in the company want to work for another person's internal search
or enterprise search.
Everybody wants to work
on hot or exciting things.
So I just said,
"Hey look, it looks like this is working, it might never really work out.
Google could kill us,
Microsoft could kill us, but we might as well try and find out." And that's how Perplexity
is functioning today.
-Very cool. So, strong product market fit
that you have, the product spreading so much
by word of mouth.
Actually, how many folks in the room today
have tried Perplexity?
Okay, so for the video, like the majority
of people in the room put their hands up.
I have used Perplexity a lot, and one of the things
I think is really amazing about the experience that
you've built is it's super fast.
How do you do that?
Well, how do you go about making
an experience like this so snappy?
-Yeah, so that's literally why the point
of us being a wrapper doesn't apply.
If you're just a wrapper,
you cannot be this fast.
And when we rolled out, we were a wrapper,
we were very slow.
Since then, we have spent a lot of work
building our own index, serving our own models.
And the third part was actually
more important than these first two.
It's orchestrating these
two things together, making sure the search call and the LLM call are happening
in parallel as much as you can.
And chunking portions of the web pages
into pieces, retrieving them really fast, and also making
a lot of asynchronous calls and trying to make sure
that the tail latencies are minimized.
By the way, all of these are concepts
you guys have put out from Google.
It's not like we have
to innovate and build, there's a whole paper from Jeff Dean
and others about why tail latencies are so important.
So we had the advantage
of building on top.
And there are two kinds
of latency improvements: actual latency improvement
and the perceived latency.
The perceived latency is
also equally important.
And that you can do through
innovation in the UX.

Perplexity's AI-powered search engine might be the next stage of interacting with the web and knowledge in general - or not. But the company is certainly risking it all to manifest that future, even if it ruffles a few feathers along the way.
Hear from Perplexity Co-Founder & CEO Aravind Srinivas on how the company plans to take on all comers in this new category of tech.

My guest today is Aravind Srinivas, the CEO of Perplexity.
Aravind is building an AI answer engine that's disrupting Google search. He gave me an inside look at how Perplexity builds products, how it scaled to 100M queries a week, and how it’ll deliver better ads than Google’s ten blue links.

Harvard was thrilled to welcome Perplexity’s co-founder and CEO Aravind Srinivas to campus for a fireside chat during the 2025 HBS Entrepreneurship Summit. In this exclusive interview, Srinivas shares his journey from electrical engineering student in India to founder of the revolutionary AI-powered search company recently valued at $9 billion.
Join Srinivas in conversation with Patrick Chung, Managing General Partner at Xfund, as he explores Srinivas’ unique path through academia to entrepreneurship, including:
– How Perplexity is transforming search and restoring confidence in AI with source citation
– Perplexity's relationship with Google, Apple, Meta, and other tech giants
– Why Perplexity is only able to plan one quarter ahead, and his surprise at DeepSeek
– How academia influenced Perplexity's approach to information
– Advice for entrepreneurs on resilience and iteration
– And a fun segment on what apps are on his phone, his favorite emoji, and what he really thinks of VC questions.
Srinivas offers candid insights on Perplexity's revenue sharing with publishers, why distillation and improved chip architecture are key to solving AI power consumption challenges, and his strategic vision for the company's future.
A must-watch for entrepreneurs, investors, and anyone interested in how AI is reshaping how we access information, from one of generative AI’s most intriguing leaders.

Is search dead? In this episode of the Superhuman AI Podcast, we sit down with Aravind Srinivas, CEO of Perplexity AI, to explore why traditional search is broken—and how AI-native answers are changing everything. From competing with Google to scaling in a multi-agent world, this is a raw look at the future of information access.
We explore:
- Why AI-native search beats Google
- The real mission behind Perplexity
- How Aravind thinks about product-market fit for AI
- What the next generation of search looks like
- If you care about AI, knowledge, and building disruptive tools—this episode is for you.

In this episode, Aravind Srinivas — Co-founder and CEO of Perplexity — joins Gobinath for a deep and insightful conversation on the future of knowledge, AI, fluid intelligence, and building meaningful technology for the world.
Aravind explains why the next era is not about knowing all answers, but about asking the right questions — and how true intelligence comes from curiosity, reasoning, and discovery.
He shares how Perplexity is built as a teacher and knowledge guide, combining the information depth of Google and Wikipedia with the conversational intelligence of ChatGPT — with a commitment to accuracy, sources, and real-time facts over ad-influenced results.
This conversation explores how Perplexity is challenging Google’s dominance, encouraging fair competition, and even expressing intent as a credible buyer for Chrome if regulators ever require divestment — all in pursuit of building a more open knowledge ecosystem.
Key Themes from the Conversation
• From knowing everything to asking better questions
• Curiosity as the only irreplaceable human ability
• Fluid intelligence as the future of learning
• India’s multilingual AI opportunity and digitising ancient knowledge
• Entrepreneurship as the best MBA — build, launch, monetize, iterate
• Intrinsic motivation in building meaningful products
• Perplexity as a teacher and knowledge guide
• Accuracy, sources and real-time facts over ads and noise
• Perplexity as a marriage of Google/Wikipedia and ChatGPT
• Breaking information bias — no ad-influenced answers
• Perplexity’s mission: be the place people come for truth and research
• Comet browser vision — search and browse like a billionaire
• “Content is king, but distribution is God” — Jio-inspired strategy
• Partnerships like Airtel to bring AI access at Indian price standards
• Challenging monopoly barriers in search
• Declaring readiness as a credible buyer for Chrome if required by DOJ — to support more competition, not replace Chrome
• Ultimate goal: democratise knowledge for everyone
This conversation explores curiosity, intelligence, ambition, and the future of information access — from a founder building it.
Stay curious. Ask better questions.

Aravind Srinivas on June 16, 2025 at AI Startup School in San Francisco.
Aravind Srinivas started Perplexity with one goal: to rethink how we search, browse, and interact with information online. In this conversation, he shares the journey from hacking together a natural-language-to-SQL search tool to building a product used by millions around the world.
He talks about the big bet on the AI-powered browser, why agents—not just chatbots—are the next step, and how speed, accuracy, and focus help a startup compete against giants. Along the way, he reflects on co-founder dynamics, early technical challenges, and what it takes to keep building when the biggest players in the world are racing alongside you.

Dr. Aravind Srinivas REVEALS Perplexity AI's Future Plans
Search is dead. Agents are rising. Phones are getting smarter—without Apple or Google.
Aravind Srinivas, CEO of @perplexity_ai, lays out the future: AI-native assistants, ambient search, agent browsers, and a war for the next-gen OS.
If Google is the old internet—Perplexity wants to be the new one.

In this View From The Top interview, Aislin Roth, MBA ‘25, speaks with Aravind Srinivas, CEO & Co-Founder of Perplexity.
Aravind shares his journey from growing up in India, moving to the U.S. to pursue his PhD in computer science, and founding Perplexity, an AI-powered “answer-engine” that recently tripled its valuation. Aravind shares his own leadership lessons, offers advice to prospective founders, and dives into what makes Perplexity unique.
A must-listen for anyone interested in entrepreneurial leadership, the future of AI, and reimagining search as we know it.

In the rapidly evolving landscape of artificial intelligence (AI), few innovations have captured the imagination quite like AI-powered search engines. In a recent interview on the Logan Bartlett Show, Aravind Srinivas, the CEO and co-founder of Perplexity AI, offered a fascinating glimpse into the future of search and the challenges of competing with tech giants like Google. Srinivas’s insights are not only relevant to the tech industry but also to anyone who uses search engines daily. Let’s delve into some of the key takeaways from this enlightening conversation.
![]()
**The Trillion-Dollar Opportunity: Knowledge on Demand**
Srinivas opens the interview by highlighting the immense potential of AI-powered search, calling it a “trillion-dollar opportunity.” He envisions a future where knowledge is not just accessible but also personalized and delivered on demand. This concept resonates deeply in today’s world, where information overload is a constant challenge. The ability to quickly and accurately find the information we need, tailored to our specific interests and needs, is a game-changer. Imagine having a personal AI assistant that can instantly answer your most pressing questions, provide relevant insights, and even anticipate your information needs. This is the future that Srinivas envisions, and it’s a future that Perplexity AI is actively working to create.
**Google’s Dilemma: User Experience vs. Shareholder Value**
One of the most intriguing aspects of the interview is Srinivas’s critique of Google’s business model. He argues that Google’s reliance on advertising revenue creates a conflict between user experience and shareholder interests. Google’s algorithms are optimized to keep users clicking on links, often leading to a frustrating search experience. This insight is particularly relevant in an era where users increasingly value efficiency and direct answers. It raises important questions about the trade-offs between profitability and user satisfaction in the tech industry. Can Google evolve its business model to prioritize user experience while maintaining its profitability? Or will new players like Perplexity AI disrupt the search landscape by putting users first?
**The Sweet Spot: Building the Ideal Search Engine**
Srinivas believes that the ideal search engine lies somewhere between traditional search results (like Google) and pure chat interfaces (like ChatGPT). He emphasizes the importance of finding the “sweet spot” that combines accuracy, high bandwidth communication, and the right form factor for consuming answers. Perplexity AI’s approach involves starting from the answer endpoint and using citations to ensure accuracy and authoritativeness. This focus on user needs and experience is a refreshing departure from the ad-driven models that dominate the search landscape. It’s a reminder that the most successful products are often those that prioritize solving real problems for users.
**The Importance of Citations: Building Trust and Accuracy**
Citations are a cornerstone of Perplexity AI’s approach. Srinivas draws inspiration from his academic background, where citations are a form of currency. He believes that a good chatbot, like a good research paper, should be backed by authoritative sources. This emphasis on transparency and reliability is crucial in an age of misinformation and fake news. By providing users with the sources of information, Perplexity AI empowers them to evaluate the credibility of the answers they receive. This commitment to transparency is not only ethically sound but also a smart business strategy, as it builds trust with users and sets Perplexity AI apart from its competitors.
**AI Safety and Regulation: A Balancing Act**
Srinivas also touches on the important topic of AI safety and regulation. He believes that while regulation is necessary, it should not be rushed. Premature regulation could stifle innovation and hinder the development of AI systems that could benefit society. This nuanced perspective is essential in the ongoing debate about AI ethics and governance. It highlights the need for thoughtful and balanced approaches that encourage innovation while ensuring safety and accountability. As AI continues to advance at a rapid pace, finding this balance will be crucial to harnessing its potential for good while mitigating its potential risks.
**The Future of AI: Models That Think for Themselves**
Looking ahead, Srinivas envisions a future where AI models can “think for themselves.” He believes that the next major breakthroughs in AI will come from models that can experiment, learn, and iterate on their own. This is a tantalizing prospect, as it suggests that AI could become an even more powerful tool for solving complex problems and generating new knowledge. However, it also raises questions about the potential risks and unintended consequences of increasingly autonomous AI systems. As we move towards this future, it’s essential to consider the ethical implications and ensure that AI is developed and used responsibly.
**The Democratization of Knowledge and Opportunity**
One of the most inspiring aspects of Srinivas’s vision is the potential for AI to democratize knowledge and opportunity. He believes that AI can empower individuals and reduce costs for businesses by automating tasks and providing access to information and expertise. This could lead to a more equitable and fulfilling world where everyone has the tools and resources to pursue their passions and create value. Imagine a world where language barriers are eliminated, where anyone can access expert advice, and where the cost of education and research is drastically reduced. This is the potential of AI, and it’s a potential that we should all be excited about.
**The Power of Iteration: A Lesson for Entrepreneurs**
Throughout the interview, Srinivas emphasizes the importance of iteration. He acknowledges that building a successful AI company is a challenging endeavor, but he believes that the key is to keep iterating, learning, and improving. This is a valuable lesson for entrepreneurs in any field. It highlights the importance of perseverance, adaptability, and a willingness to embrace failure as a learning opportunity. In the fast-paced world of AI, the ability to learn and adapt quickly is essential for survival and success.
**Conclusion: A Glimpse into a Transformative Future**
Srinivas’s interview offers a captivating glimpse into the future of search and the transformative potential of AI. His insights challenge us to rethink our relationship with information and technology. While the path ahead is uncertain, one thing is clear: AI is poised to play an increasingly central role in our lives. Whether it’s through personalized knowledge on demand, more efficient search engines, or AI models that can think for themselves, the possibilities are both exciting and daunting.
As we navigate this uncharted territory, it’s crucial to engage in thoughtful conversations about the ethical implications and societal impact of AI. Srinivas’s interview is a valuable contribution to this ongoing dialogue. It reminds us that the future of AI is not just about technological advancements but also about how we choose to use this powerful tool to shape our world.
If you’re curious to learn more about the future of search and the fascinating world of AI, I encourage you to check out the full interview with Aravind Srinivas. It’s a thought-provoking conversation that will leave you pondering the possibilities and challenges that lie ahead.

Human potential will only accelerate with AI answering questions better and faster than ever before, says Aravind Srinivas, cofounder and CEO of conversational search engine Perplexity. He examines the trends driving new AI-powered tools that nourish curiosity and creativity — and how they might usher in a new era of intellectual growth and discovery. "Knowledge does not really care about who you are, where you're from or who you have access to. Rather, what matters is the next question you're going to ask," says Srinivas. (Recorded at TEDAI San Francisco on October 22, 2024)

AI Native 2025 Fireside Chat | Perplexity Founder and CEO, Aravind Srinivas
Aravind Srinivas is the co-founder and CEO of Perplexity AI, a leading company specializing in conversational AI-driven search. Born in Chennai, India, he holds dual degrees in electrical engineering from IIT Madras and a PhD in computer science from UC Berkeley. Before founding Perplexity in 2022, Aravind worked at OpenAI, Google, and DeepMind, where he contributed to major advances in machine learning. Under his leadership, Perplexity has rapidly grown into one of the world’s fastest-scaling AI startups, backed by top investors and recognized for its innovative approach to delivering direct, cited answers to complex questions.
About AI Native
Zetta Venture Partners launched AI Native with a clear vision: to create the defining forum where researchers, founders, and practitioners come together to shape the future of artificial intelligence. Unlike generic conferences, AI Native is rooted in the conviction that the next generation of breakthrough companies will be AI-native at their core—businesses built with AI as the fundamental building block, not an afterthought.
AI Native 2025 carries that mission forward with a powerhouse lineup, featuring Aravind Srinivas (Perplexity), Aparna Chennapragada (Microsoft), Daphne Koller (Insitro, Coursera), Liam Fedus (Periodic Labs), and leaders from OpenAI, Sierra, Glean, Harvey, Browserbase, Motherduck, Domino Data Labs, Noetik, LILT, and many more.
Building on the momentum of last year’s summit—with standout keynotes from Ion Stoica (Databricks), Sean Lie (Cerebras), Erik Brynjolfsson (Stanford), Mike Schroepfer (Meta), among others—AI Native 2025 will dive deeper into the technical breakthroughs and real-world applications that are redefining how AI is built, deployed, and scaled across industries.

If AI could give everyone the same edge billionaires have, what would the world look like?
I sat down with Aravind Srinivas, CEO and co-founder of Perplexity AI, the $20B startup reimagining how we search, learn, and get things done.
Aravind believes AI shouldn’t just answer your questions but rather it should think with you.
In this conversation, we explore how Perplexity’s new assistant Comet acts as your second brain, doing the tasks billionaires hire entire teams for.
From booking travel and running marketing campaigns to handling customer support and organizing your life, Comet gives everyone the same leverage the ultra-wealthy already have.
We also dive into:
🧠 How “second-brain” AI changes human creativity
⚙️ Why culture and product design must mirror each other
📚 How AI could democratize academic research
💻 What the future of on-device AI means for privacy and performance
🚀 And how Perplexity is quietly challenging the world’s biggest search engines
This isn’t just about AI... it’s about access, time, and the future of human potential.
Timestamps:
About the Guest:
Aravind Srinivas is the co-founder and CEO of Perplexity AI, the company building AI search and assistant technologies used by millions worldwide. Previously at OpenAI, DeepMind, and Google Research, his mission is to make knowledge and productivity tools accessible to everyone.
About This Series:
Talk Tech with Tiff brings you inside the minds of the world’s leading builders and innovators. From AI to robotics, each episode explores the technologies shaping our future and the people making it happen.

You’re an intern at OpenAI, then Deep Mind, followed by Google…
Years later, you’re a Research Scientist at OpenAI, which you quit after a year to bet on yourself.
Cut to 2024.
You’ve built a $1B AI-based search company with investors like Nvidia, Daniel Gross, Jeff Bezos, & more.
That’s Aravind Srinivas, CEO of Perplexity.ai’s story.
In this episode of Product-Led AI, Aravind and I discuss a host of topics– Perplexity’s moat as the AI-powered "answer engine" that is disrupting search, how this could impact the ad-based business model of the sector, why startups have an edge on incumbents, and more.
You can read a transcript of our conversation and find more about the series at https://productledaipod.com/

Aravind Srinivas and Perplexity AI: The Audacious Challenge to Google’s Search Empire | by Gene Dai | Medium
